stories.headline: Measles exposure reported at Broomfield High School and multiple Front Range locations
stories.summary:
Students and staff who were at Broomfield High School on Feb. 19, Feb. 20 or Feb. 23 may have been exposed to measles, Colorado public health officials announced Friday. The case involves an unvaccinated child between the ages of 5 and 17 who had not recently traveled outside Colorado or had contact with a known infected person, suggesting unidentified cases may be circulating in the area.

Additional exposure sites include a King Soopers in Broomfield, a PetSmart in Westminster, a restaurant in Lafayette and medical facilities in Broomfield and Louisville. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment urged anyone who was at those locations during specified times to monitor for symptoms, which begin like a common cold with fever, cough, runny nose and red eyes.

This is the second confirmed measles case in a Colorado resident in 2026. Earlier this week, an out-of-state infected adult traveled through Denver International Airport and attended a church service in Littleton, potentially exposing others. Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, its highest total in decades, and nearly 1,000 cases have already been reported nationally in 2026.

stories.headline: Denver report on restaurant industry sets stage for tipped-wage policy change; councilman to propose measure by end of March
stories.summary:
A new report released Friday by Denver restaurant industry advocates details the economic struggles facing local eateries and recommends ways the city might provide relief, laying the groundwork for a change to Denver's tipped-wage policy. City Councilman Darrell Watson confirmed he plans to bring a proposal to fellow council members before the end of March, though he declined to provide specifics.

The report, authored by city-appointed restaurant liaisons Adam Schlegel and Dana Faulk Query, found that labor costs were the most frequently cited challenge among restaurant operators and the most destabilizing factor for full-service establishments. Federal labor data cited in the report shows Denver had 15% fewer full-service restaurant jobs in 2025 than at the start of 2020.

Under current city rules, employers of tipped workers can offset wages by up to $3.02 per hour, meaning they must pay at least $16.27 per hour while workers must reach the full $19.29 minimum wage through tips. Any reduction to the required tipped wage would keep workers at minimum wage but shift more of that burden onto tips, effectively lowering take-home pay — a point that has drawn fierce opposition from labor unions at the state level.

stories.headline: Measles case in Broomfield — cyclist killed on Highway 119 in Boulder County in collision with dump truck
stories.summary:
A male cyclist was killed Friday morning after a collision with a dump truck on Highway 119 near Niwot Road in Boulder County, the Colorado State Patrol reported. The crash occurred around 11 a.m. and the man was pronounced dead at the scene; no other injuries were reported.

Highway 119 was closed at Airport Road following the crash while investigators worked the scene. The State Patrol provided limited details and said the investigation was ongoing.

The fatal crash comes amid a week of heightened traffic and safety concerns along Front Range roadways, with multiple Red Flag Warning days contributing to dangerous conditions across the region.

stories.headline: Update: Thornton wildfire destroyed approximately 150 BMWs at luxury auto shop; cause still unknown
stories.summary:
About 150 BMW vehicles were destroyed when the Huron fire swept through 10 acres in Thornton on Wednesday, with the blaze heavily damaging Rol Race Autosports, a specialty BMW repair and parts shop that draws customers from across the country. Shop owner Jason Rol said many of the cars belonged to enthusiasts working to restore rare vehicles, and he described himself as being "in shock" as he assessed the full scope of the loss.

Approximately 150 firefighters responded to the blaze, which started near Pinnacle Charter High School and spread rapidly through dry conditions within seconds of ignition. Three firefighters and one fire investigator sustained minor injuries. As of Friday, the cause of the fire remained undetermined and officials could not provide a timeline for when that determination might be made.

The GoFundMe campaign started by Rol's shop had raised $6,000 as of Friday afternoon. Five businesses in total were affected, most of them auto services. The fire was one of four separate blazes to strike the Denver metro area this week amid persistent Red Flag Warning conditions.

stories.headline: Douglas County brush fire scorches 8 acres near Parker, fourth Denver-area blaze this week
stories.summary:
A brush fire in Douglas County grew to about 8 acres Friday morning in the 7800 block of Merryvale Trail, southeast of Parker, before South Metro Fire Rescue crews brought it under control. The fire threatened several homes and outbuildings, prompting brief evacuation orders on Vinegarroon Road and Sonoma Trail before they were lifted as crews gained the upper hand.

Authorities received the initial 911 call just before 10:30 a.m. Investigators have not determined a cause, but noted the blaze moved quickly in dry conditions. Requests for air support were ultimately canceled.

The incident marks the fourth new wildfire to ignite in the Denver area this week. Earlier blazes included a roughly 500-acre Douglas County grassland fire on Tuesday, a 10-acre Thornton fire on Wednesday and a Jefferson County house fire that spread to nearby vegetation on Thursday. The entire Front Range remained under a Red Flag Warning Friday due to extreme fire conditions, high temperatures and strong wind gusts.

stories.headline: Colorado Avalanche lead shrinks to five points in Central Division as Wild, Stars surge
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche's commanding lead in the NHL Central Division has eroded significantly, with the club going just 7-8-2 since a historic 31-2-7 start to the season. A 5-2 loss to the Minnesota Wild on Thursday dropped Colorado's lead to five points over the Wild, with two games in hand, and six points over the Dallas Stars with one game in hand.

Coach Jared Bednar characterized Thursday's loss as a missed opportunity, noting that a win would have pushed the lead to nine points with two games in hand. Minnesota and Dallas have combined for a 14-0-1 record since Jan. 22, reversing the midseason funks that had allowed Colorado to build its cushion.

The schedule will present further challenges: the Avs face 27 games in 51 days following the Olympic break, including a four-point clash with Minnesota at Ball Arena on March 8, three remaining games against Dallas and a stretch of critical division matchups. Avs captain Gabe Landeskog acknowledged the importance of the upcoming games while urging the team to stay focused on its own performance.

stories.headline: Broncos place second-round tender on cornerback Ja'Quan McMillian, locking him in for 2026
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are placing a second-round restricted free agent tender worth $5.8 million on cornerback Ja'Quan McMillian, all but ensuring he will return for the 2026 season. McMillian's agent, Deryk Gilmore, confirmed the move to The Denver Post on Friday at the NFL Scouting Combine in Indianapolis, saying the Broncos notified his client Thursday night.

Because a team would have to surrender a second-round draft pick to sign McMillian away, it is considered highly unlikely any franchise will attempt to do so. McMillian posted the best season of his NFL career in 2025, recording four sacks, 61 tackles, two interceptions and nine passes defensed while playing 30% or fewer defensive snaps in nickel and dime packages.

The Broncos now face a longer-term question about their cornerback room, which includes Pat Surtain, Riley Moss, McMillian and 2025 first-round pick Jahdae Barron. Gilmore suggested Denver may look to move Barron to outside corner, with Moss potentially becoming available via trade at midseason. Both Moss and McMillian will be unrestricted free agents after the upcoming season.

stories.headline: Nuggets clear Jamal Murray to play against Thunder despite illness; Denver seeks first winning streak since January
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets upgraded Jamal Murray to available Friday for their game against the Oklahoma City Thunder after he had been listed as questionable due to illness. Murray had checked out early in Denver's Feb. 25 win over the Boston Celtics after less than eight minutes, with coach David Adelman saying Murray "looked awful" and that medication had not helped.

The Nuggets enter Friday's matchup holding a 37-22 record, less than one game behind the third-seeded Houston Rockets in the Western Conference. Denver is looking to build on a 103-84 victory over Boston and string together its first winning streak since late January.

The Thunder, along with the San Antonio Spurs, hold one of the top two seeds in the Western Conference. Denver lost to Oklahoma City on Feb. 1, with Murray posting 12 points and 12 assists in a losing effort. Friday's game tips off at 9:30 p.m. ET.

stories.headline: Rockies beat Giants 11-3 in spring training; players discuss new hitting approach
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies defeated the San Francisco Giants 11-3 in spring training action Friday, with several players turning in notable performances. Outfielder Jordan Beck drew attention for an exceptional throw, while Kyle Karros hit a triple and spoke publicly about the team's new hitting philosophy.

Manager Warren Schaeffer and outfield prospect Cole Carrigg also commented on the team's performance and spring training progress. Carrigg addressed his development through camp, signaling optimism heading into the 2026 season.

The Rockies, coming off several difficult seasons, have emphasized an overhauled approach at the plate under new hitting instruction. The lopsided spring win offers an early positive sign as the club works to rebuild ahead of Opening Day.

stories.headline: RTD Transit Police launch patrols from new substations in Englewood, Boulder to cut response times
stories.summary:
Regional Transportation District Transit Police began operating from two new substations Friday — one at the Elati light rail facility in Englewood and one at the Downtown Boulder Station — aimed at increasing officer visibility and shortening response times across the eight-county service area. Officers will use the locations as patrol bases seven days a week, supplementing existing districtwide coverage rather than replacing central dispatching.

The Englewood substation positions officers closer to southern metro stations such as RidgeGate and Littleton, while the Boulder substation enhances presence at the downtown bus terminal and surrounding community. RTD Police Chief Steve Martingano said the substations will allow more efficient travel to key areas compared with dispatching from downtown Denver.

The two sites serve as a pilot program; officials plan to evaluate results before pursuing additional substations, including a potential site near the W and G light rail lines. RTD noted that security-related incident reports dropped roughly 18% in 2025 and customer reports of illicit drug activity fell 51% in January 2026 compared with January 2025, citing staffing increases and 24/7 patrols implemented in May 2024.

stories.headline: Colorado lieutenant governor tapped as interim head of state behavioral health agency following commissioner's abrupt retirement
stories.summary:
Colorado's behavioral health agency has appointed an interim director following the abrupt retirement of its commissioner, marking the second leadership transition at the agency in four years. The lieutenant governor was tapped to lead the Colorado Behavioral Health Administration on an interim basis while a permanent replacement is sought.

The agency oversees the state's mental health and substance use disorder services, a sector that has faced significant scrutiny in recent years amid rising demand and concerns about service gaps. The leadership change comes as Colorado continues to grapple with a statewide behavioral health crisis that has prompted multiple legislative and budgetary responses.

Details on the circumstances surrounding the commissioner's retirement and a timeline for selecting a permanent replacement were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools offers crossing guards self-defense training as driver aggression increases
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools has launched its first self-defense training program for its 45 school crossing guards after district officials said incidents of aggression from drivers and pedestrians have become more frequent in recent years. Andrea Garcia, who manages the crossing guard program, said the problem has worsened significantly since the COVID-19 pandemic, with drivers increasingly getting out of their cars to confront guards.

The training — two years in the making — teaches guards how to strike, when to move and how to use pressure points as a last resort if de-escalation techniques fail. A new requirement also mandates that guards report every aggressive incident to dispatch, allowing the district to begin systematically tracking the frequency of such encounters.

The initiative comes after an incident at Mapleton Public Schools in Thornton in which a crossing guard was stabbed outside an elementary school. Garcia said the event deeply affected her. Crossing guard Amié Williams said the training has given her a new level of confidence while on duty at KIPP Northeast Campus, where her own children attend school.

weather.forecast: It's gonna be 68 degrees today in Denver. That's February. I don't know what to tell you. It's gonna be cloudy though, which makes sense, because the sun saw 68 degrees in February and said you know what, I'm not even gonna take credit for this one. Winds up to 15 miles per hour, which is just enough to make you think something's about to happen, and then nothing happens.
